sources: UK FSA Traffic Light Nutrient Profiling System
WHO Global Action Plan for NCDs 2013-2030
ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes 2023

field_legend.max_*: Upper safe limit per 100g — lower is better
field_legend.min_*: Minimum desirable level per 100g — higher is better
field_legend.note: Fields marked with [RESTRICTION] mean nutrient must be LIMITED, not encouraged

general._fixes: Original values were correct. Added trans fat, total fat, cholesterol, carbs, fiber, and calorie caps per UK FSA Traffic Light System and FDA 2020-2025 Guidelines.

diabetic._fixes: Original values were correct. Added carb cap, glycemic load, trans fat, and fiber — all critical for blood sugar management per ADA Standards of Care 2023.

heart._fixes: FIXED: max_sugar_g reduced from 15 → 10 (AHA 2021 guidelines are stricter for cardiac patients). Added cholesterol cap (50mg), trans fat (0.1g), omega-3 minimum, and total fat cap per AHA/ACC Prevention Guidelines 2019.

pcos._fixes: Original values were correct. Added carb cap, glycemic load, and fiber — essential for managing insulin resistance in PCOS per Endocrine Society CPG 2023.

hypertension._source: NIH/NHLBI DASH Diet; AHA 2021 Hypertension Guidelines. Sodium ≤120mg/100g = UK FSA 'green' (low). DASH target: <1500mg sodium/day. Potassium minimum counteracts sodium's blood-pressure-raising effect.

high_cholesterol._source: AHA/ACC 2019 Primary Prevention Guidelines; NICE Lipid Modification CG181. Sat fat <7% total kcal to reduce LDL; dietary cholesterol <200mg/day for hypercholesterolemia; soluble fiber 5-10g/day lowers LDL 3-5%.

hypertriglyceridemia._source: AHA/ACC Triglyceride Management Guidelines 2021. Refined carbs and fructose are primary dietary drivers of elevated TG. Alcohol zero-tolerance for TG >500 mg/dL.

metabolic_syndrome._source: IDF/AHA/NHLBI Joint Scientific Statement on Metabolic Syndrome; AHA Metabolic Syndrome Dietary Guidance 2021. Combined criteria address abdominal obesity, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL, hypertension, and insulin resistance.

obesity._source: WHO Obesity Fact Sheet 2022; NICE CG189 (Obesity). Energy density <2 kcal/g preferred. High protein and fiber promote satiety. 5-10% weight loss improves all metabolic markers significantly.

fatty_liver_nafld._source: EASL NAFLD Guidelines 2016; AGA Clinical Practice Guideline on NAFLD 2023. Fructose drives de novo lipogenesis directly in hepatocytes. Mediterranean diet is first-line. 5-10% weight loss reduces hepatic steatosis by 30-40%.

liver_cirrhosis._source: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines on Nutrition in Chronic Liver Disease 2019; ESPEN Guideline on Clinical Nutrition in Liver Disease 2019. Sodium <2000mg/day for ascites. Protein 1.2-1.5g/kg/day to prevent sarcopenia.

ckd_stage_3_4._note: max_protein_g is a RESTRICTION — protein must be LIMITED in non-dialysis CKD to slow progression.
ckd_stage_3_4._source: KDIGO 2024 CKD Guidelines; NKF KDOQI Nutrition Guidelines 2020. Protein 0.6-0.8g/kg/day (non-dialysis). Phosphorus <800mg/day. Potassium individualized, typically <2000-2500mg/day.

kidney_stones._note: max_protein_g is a RESTRICTION — excess animal protein acidifies urine and raises oxalate/uric acid excretion.
kidney_stones._source: AUA/EAU Urolithiasis Guidelines 2022; NIH NIDDK Kidney Stones Diet Guide. High sodium increases urinary calcium excretion. Fluid intake >2.5L/day is primary intervention.

pancreatitis._source: IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for Chronic Pancreatitis 2020; ESPEN Pancreatitis Guideline 2020. Total fat <30g/day. MCT oils better tolerated. Protein 1.0-1.5g/kg/day to prevent malnutrition.

gout._source: ACR 2020 Guideline for the Management of Gout. Fructose uniquely increases uric acid via AMP deamination. Organ meats, shellfish, red meat (>150mg purine/100g) must be avoided. Dairy protein is uricosuric — dairy encouraged.

celiac_disease._note: max_gluten_mg: 20mg/100g = 20 ppm — legal threshold for 'gluten-free' labelling in both EU and USA.
celiac_disease._source: EU Reg. 41/2009; FDA Gluten-Free Labeling Rule (21 CFR Part 101.91); Codex Alimentarius CODEX STAN 118-1979 (rev. 2015). Iron minimum added as deficiency anemia is common in newly diagnosed celiac.

ibs._source: Monash University Low-FODMAP Diet Guidelines 2023; NICE IBS Guideline CG61 (reviewed 2023). FODMAP = Fermentable Oligosaccharides, Disaccharides, Monosaccharides And Polyols. Effective in 75% of IBS patients.

crohns_disease._note: min_fiber_g intentionally low — insoluble fiber worsens Crohn's flares and stricture-related obstruction.
crohns_disease._source: ECCO-EFCCA Crohn's Disease Guidelines 2020; ESPEN IBD Guideline 2017. Low fat reduces diarrhea. Protein 1.2-1.5g/kg/day to counter malabsorption.

ulcerative_colitis._source: ECCO IBD Guidelines 2022; ESPEN IBD Clinical Nutrition Guideline 2017. Soluble fiber tolerated better than insoluble. Omega-3 EPA/DHA has anti-inflammatory benefit.

osteoporosis._source: NOF Clinician's Guide to Prevention and Treatment of Osteoporosis 2022; IOF Nutrition and Bone Health Position Paper 2023. Each extra 500mg Na = ~10mg urinary calcium loss. Protein 1.0-1.2g/kg/day maintains bone matrix. Calcium DRI: 1000-1200mg/day.

iron_deficiency_anemia._source: WHO Nutritional Anaemia Guidelines 2020; EFSA Iron DRV 2015. Vitamin C increases non-heme iron absorption 2-3x. Inhibitors: calcium, polyphenols (tea/coffee), phytates. Iron RDA: 8mg/day (men), 18mg/day (women), 27mg/day (pregnancy).

hypothyroidism._source: ATA Patient Resources; ETA Guidelines for Hypothyroidism 2022. Hypothyroidism reduces BMR by 15-30% — strict calorie control prevents weight gain. Selenium (55mcg/day) supports deiodinase enzyme activity.

hyperthyroidism._source: ATA Hyperthyroidism Guidelines 2016 (updated). Hyperthyroidism raises BMR 20-80% — higher caloric and protein intake prevents catabolism. max_iodine added: excess iodine can precipitate thyroid storms.

hypothyroidism_hashimotos._source: AACE/ACE Thyroid Disease Clinical Practice Guidelines 2022; Thyroid (journal) autoimmune dietary reviews 2020-2023. Gluten may worsen intestinal permeability and autoimmune thyroid response. Selenium reduces TPO antibody levels. Anti-inflammatory diet pattern (Mediterranean) recommended.

pregnancy._source: ACOG Nutrition During Pregnancy; WHO Antenatal Care Recommendations 2016; EFSA DRVs in Pregnancy 2019. Folate 600mcg/day DFE; Iron 27mg/day; avoid high-mercury fish, alcohol, unpasteurized products. max_vitamin_a: excess (>10,000 IU/day) is teratogenic.

gestational_diabetes._source: ACOG Practice Bulletin on GDM 2018; ADA Standards of Care GDM Section 2023; NICE NG3 Diabetes in Pregnancy 2023. Breakfast carbs most restricted due to morning insulin resistance. Low GI diet reduces pharmacotherapy need by ~30%.

lactation._source: WHO/UNICEF Infant Feeding Recommendations 2022; ACOG Postpartum Nutrition; EFSA DRVs for Lactating Women 2019. Additional 400-500 kcal/day needed. Iodine critical for infant brain development (290mcg/day maternal target).

depression_mental_health._source: SMILES Trial (Jacka et al., BMC Medicine 2017); MIND Diet Guidelines (Morris et al. 2015); APA Task Force on Nutrition and Mental Health 2021. Ultra-processed foods and high added sugar increase depression risk 25-35%. Mediterranean diet reduces depression risk 30-50%.

autoimmune_general._source: Autoimmune Protocol (AIP) Diet — Konijeti et al. 2017; ACR Rheumatoid Arthritis Dietary Guidance 2022. High sodium activates Th17 pro-inflammatory pathways (Wilck et al., Nature 2017). Anti-inflammatory Mediterranean-pattern diet is evidence-based for RA and SLE.

lactose_intolerance._source: NIH Consensus Development Conference: Lactose Intolerance and Health 2010; EFSA Lactose Threshold Opinion 2010. Most adults tolerate up to 12g lactose/day. ≤1g/100g = effectively 'lactose-free', safe even for severe cases. Calcium minimum added as dairy avoidance risks deficiency.

acne._source: AAD Acne and Diet Position Statement 2022; Fiedler et al. 2017 (JEADV). High-GI diet increases IGF-1 and androgens → sebum overproduction. Milk/whey protein increases IGF-1. Low-GI diet reduces acne lesion count by 22% (Smith et al., Am J Clin Nutr).

migraine._source: American Headache Society Dietary Triggers Review 2021; IHS Dietary Management Guidelines. Common triggers: tyramine (aged cheeses, cured meats), histamine (wine, fermented foods), MSG, aspartame. Magnesium deficiency linked to migraine frequency — 400-600mg/day supplementation evidence-based.

elderly_65_plus._source: ESPEN Practical Guideline on Nutrition and Ageing 2022; NIA/NIH Dietary Guidelines for Older Adults; PROT-AGE Study Group. Protein 1.0-1.2g/kg/day prevents sarcopenia. Caloric needs decrease ~100-200 kcal/decade but micronutrient needs remain. Vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day for fall prevention. B12 absorption decreases with age — minimum added.

athletic_active._source: ISSN Position Stands 2017-2023; IOC Consensus Statement on RED-S 2023; ADA/DC/ACSM Joint Position Statement 2016 (updated). Protein 1.4-2.0g/kg/day. Carbs 5-12g/kg/day by training load. Higher sugar tolerance pre/intra-exercise as rapid fuel source.

pediatric_2_to_12._source: AAP Dietary Guidelines for Children 2022; EFSA DRVs for Children 2013-2017; WHO Free Sugars Guideline 2015. WHO: free sugars <10% total energy. Sodium DRI: 1000mg/day (2-3yr) → 1500mg/day (9-12yr). Adequate calcium critical for peak bone mass development.

adolescent_13_to_17._source: AAP Adolescent Nutrition Guidelines; EFSA DRVs for Adolescents; CDC Adolescent and School Health Nutrition. Calcium 1300mg/day — highest of any life stage (peak bone mass accrual). Iron needs increase significantly at menarche.
